html:
Brita Olofsdotter, who died in 1569, was a Swedish cavalry soldier in the Swedish army who served in the Livonian War. She is perhaps the oldest historically documented example in Sweden of women disguising themselves as men to serve in professions reserved for men in Europe; she can also be considered Sweden's first female soldier.

Olofsdotter came from Finland and was a widow, having been married to Nils Simonsson. She disguised herself as a man and enlisted in the army during the Livonian War, where she served in the cavalry and died in battle. On June 16, 1569, Johan III informed Gabriel Christensson about the matter, ordered an investigation, and instructed that her remaining salary should be paid to her relatives.

== References == Eva Borgström: Extraordinary Women; gender-crossing figures in myth and reality, ISBN 9789150101911, Alfabet (2002). Brita Olofsdotter in Wilhelmina Stålberg, Anteckningar om svenska qvinnor (1864).

How certain are we about this?

From the Vasa era onwards, individuals are well-documented in archives. The uncertainty lies more in interpretation and how later generations perceived events – not least because those in power themselves shaped historical narratives.
tldr: Brita Olofsdotter was a woman who, in the 16th century, disguised herself as a man to fight as a soldier in wars. She is considered one of the first female soldiers in Swedish history.
lattlast:
Brita Olofsdotter was a woman from Finland who died in battle in 1569. At that time, women were not allowed to serve as soldiers, but she disguised herself as a man to join the army and fight in the cavalry.

When she died in combat, her death came to the attention of King John III. He ordered an investigation into what had happened and decided that the money she had earned during her service in the army should be paid to her family.

Historians have found documents that confirm her life and achievements. Today, she is considered one of the earliest known examples of a woman in Europe who took on a profession that was then only permitted for men.
